IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—populating fine-grained IsA classes unseen verbatim—and introduces a novel, linguistically motivated approach (modifier property profiles and composition) that produces substantial empirical gains (>10-point AUC over a strong Hearst baseline on Wikipedia category reconstruction). Its claim to be the first to leverage noun-compound semantic interpretations for downstream IsA extraction suggests it will influence follow-up work. However, dependence on in-house fact extractions, limited hyperparameter and code transparency, and acknowledged coverage limitations mean the contribution is strong but not yet fully transformative without clearer reproducibility and robustness evidence. | B(4): This work tackles a meaningful gap—populating fine-grained, compositional IsA classes (e.g., “1950s American jazz composers”)—by leveraging modifier-level property profiles learned from large extracted fact repositories, which is a useful conceptual contribution beyond standard Hearst-pattern approaches. The empirical gains (10+ point AUC improvement reconstructing Wikipedia categories) and claim of being the first to operationalize noun-compound semantic interpretation for downstream IsA extraction suggest the approach could shift how researchers model modifier semantics for taxonomy induction. While applicability depends on the coverage of extracted facts and the subsective-modifier assumption, the paper opens clear new directions for compositional class induction and richer lexical-resource construction that others are likely to build on.

Its claim to be the first to leverage noun-compound semantic interpretations for downstream IsA extraction suggests it will influence follow-up work. However, dependence on in-house fact extractions, limited hyperparameter and code transparency, and acknowledged coverage limitations mean the contribution is strong but not yet fully transformative without clearer reproducibility and robustness evidence.

The empirical gains (10+ point AUC improvement reconstructing Wikipedia categories) and claim of being the first to operationalize noun-compound semantic interpretation for downstream IsA extraction suggest the approach could shift how researchers model modifier semantics for taxonomy induction. While applicability depends on the coverage of extracted facts and the subsective-modifier assumption, the paper opens clear new directions for compositional class induction and richer lexical-resource construction that others are likely to build on.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (fine-grained IsA extraction and taxonomy population) and proposes methods and evaluations (AUC on reconstructing Wikipedia categories) that are squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The approach—modifier property profiles learned from large relation extractions and composition for unseen class queries—is well targeted to the community, but the empirical setup raises reproducibility and rigor concerns (in‑house ReVerb/OLLIE repository, no code or hyperparameter reporting) that temper a perfect fit. With clearer experimental artifacts (data splits, hyperparameters, code) and stronger ablations/statistical analyses the work would be an excellent match. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it tackles fine-grained IsA extraction and taxonomy induction using a linguistically motivated approach (modifier property profiles and composition) that should interest the NLP/semantic-systems community. The evaluation on Wikipedia category reconstruction against a strong Hearst-patterns baseline with AUC reporting and the claim of applying noun-compound semantics to downstream IsA extraction match the methodological expectations of the venue. While some implementation details (e.g., in-house ReVerb/OLLIE facts, code/hyperparameters) could be clearer, those do not undermine the paper’s relevance or audience fit for a top-tier conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (fine-grained IsA extraction and taxonomy population) and proposes methods and evaluations (AUC on reconstructing Wikipedia categories) that are squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The approach—modifier property profiles learned from large relation extractions and composition for unseen class queries—is well targeted to the community, but the empirical setup raises reproducibility and rigor concerns (in‑house ReVerb/OLLIE repository, no code or hyperparameter reporting) that temper a perfect fit. With clearer experimental artifacts (data splits, hyperparameters, code) and stronger ablations/statistical analyses the work would be an excellent match.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it tackles fine-grained IsA extraction and taxonomy induction using a linguistically motivated approach (modifier property profiles and composition) that should interest the NLP/semantic-systems community. The evaluation on Wikipedia category reconstruction against a strong Hearst-patterns baseline with AUC reporting and the claim of applying noun-compound semantics to downstream IsA extraction match the methodological expectations of the venue. While some implementation details (e.g., in-house ReVerb/OLLIE facts, code/hyperparameters) could be clearer, those do not undermine the paper’s relevance or audience fit for a top-tier conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper compares against a strong Hearst-patterns baseline but omits several directly relevant prior approaches to hyponymy/taxonomy induction (e.g., semantic taxonomy induction from heterogeneous evidence and classic hyponym acquisition methods) and lacks comparisons to more recent or heterogeneous baselines that target fine-grained IsA extraction. It is unclear whether all methods were evaluated under identical data and preprocessing conditions (the summary does not report shared splits or hyperparameter controls), so fairness of comparisons is not fully supported. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and acknowledge limitations, but they do not demonstrate when prior methods perform comparably or provide detailed analyses isolating why their method outperforms the single baseline. Given these gaps, the empirical comparison is useful but incomplete. | B(3): The paper evaluates against a strong Hearst-patterns baseline on the same Wikipedia category reconstruction task and reports AUC improvements, so comparisons are partly fair. However, it appears to omit other directly relevant and more recent taxonomy/IsA induction approaches (e.g., heterogeneous-evidence taxonomy induction and classic hyponymy acquisition work), so key baselines are missing. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly, but do not appear to benchmark against or acknowledge when alternative methods perform comparably, limiting the empirical situating of their contribution. Overall the comparisons are reasonable but incomplete for convincing claims of superiority.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong Hearst-patterns baseline but omits several directly relevant prior approaches to hyponymy/taxonomy induction (e.g., semantic taxonomy induction from heterogeneous evidence and classic hyponym acquisition methods) and lacks comparisons to more recent or heterogeneous baselines that target fine-grained IsA extraction. It is unclear whether all methods were evaluated under identical data and preprocessing conditions (the summary does not report shared splits or hyperparameter controls), so fairness of comparisons is not fully supported. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly and acknowledge limitations, but they do not demonstrate when prior methods perform comparably or provide detailed analyses isolating why their method outperforms the single baseline. Given these gaps, the empirical comparison is useful but incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against a strong Hearst-patterns baseline on the same Wikipedia category reconstruction task and reports AUC improvements, so comparisons are partly fair. However, it appears to omit other directly relevant and more recent taxonomy/IsA induction approaches (e.g., heterogeneous-evidence taxonomy induction and classic hyponymy acquisition work), so key baselines are missing. The authors do discuss related work thoroughly, but do not appear to benchmark against or acknowledge when alternative methods perform comparably, limiting the empirical situating of their contribution. Overall the comparisons are reasonable but incomplete for convincing claims of superiority.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper makes clear, testable claims and presents empirical gains (10+ point AUC improvement vs. a Hearst-patterns baseline) which supports the central idea, but several methodological gaps weaken confidence. Key experimental details are missing (no code, hyperparameter settings, dataset splits, or statistical significance testing) and the approach depends on an in-house fact repository and distributional assumptions that are acknowledged but not empirically ablated. From a reproducibility and rigor standpoint I would expect ablations isolating modifier profiles, sensitivity analyses to fact-repo coverage, and confidence intervals before accepting all conclusions. | B(3): The paper states clear claims and provides experimental evidence (a >10-point AUC gain reconstructing Wikipedia category pages) that supports the core idea of composing modifier property profiles for fine-grained IsA extraction. However, from the summary there are gaps in methodological detail: no hyperparameters or code are reported, comparison is limited to a single Hearst-patterns baseline, and statistical significance or additional controls are not mentioned. The approach also rests on acknowledged but strong assumptions (modifier subsectivity and heavy dependence on noisy ReVerb/OLLIE extractions) that are not fully tested, leaving some uncertainty about generality. Overall the technique appears promising but the experimental validation and ablation of assumptions are incomplete, so I rate it a 3.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims and presents empirical gains (10+ point AUC improvement vs. a Hearst-patterns baseline) which supports the central idea, but several methodological gaps weaken confidence. Key experimental details are missing (no code, hyperparameter settings, dataset splits, or statistical significance testing) and the approach depends on an in-house fact repository and distributional assumptions that are acknowledged but not empirically ablated. From a reproducibility and rigor standpoint I would expect ablations isolating modifier profiles, sensitivity analyses to fact-repo coverage, and confidence intervals before accepting all conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experimental evidence (a >10-point AUC gain reconstructing Wikipedia category pages) that supports the core idea of composing modifier property profiles for fine-grained IsA extraction. However, from the summary there are gaps in methodological detail: no hyperparameters or code are reported, comparison is limited to a single Hearst-patterns baseline, and statistical significance or additional controls are not mentioned. The approach also rests on acknowledged but strong assumptions (modifier subsectivity and heavy dependence on noisy ReVerb/OLLIE extractions) that are not fully tested, leaving some uncertainty about generality. Overall the technique appears promising but the experimental validation and ablation of assumptions are incomplete, so I rate it a 3.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (modifier composition for fine-grained IsA extraction, modeling modifiers as property profiles), making the narrative easy to follow from method to experiments. Notation appears consistent (e.g., use of φ̂ for property profiles) and the methods section explains how modifier distributions are learned and combined; figures/tables and examples reportedly support the AUC-based results (10+ point gain) and the reconstruction task. Writing quality is good and related work is thorough, but presentation could be tightened around experimental reproducibility details (no mention of code or hyperparameters in the summary), which slightly reduces clarity for readers wanting to replicate results. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (composition of modifier property-profiles for fine-grained IsA extraction). The methodology and experiments follow a logical flow and limitations/assumptions are explicitly discussed, which aids clarity. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective, and writing quality is described as good, though a few minor presentation details (e.g., explicit reporting of notation/hyperparameters and some methodological specifics) could be tightened for full clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (modifier composition for fine-grained IsA extraction, modeling modifiers as property profiles), making the narrative easy to follow from method to experiments. Notation appears consistent (e.g., use of φ̂ for property profiles) and the methods section explains how modifier distributions are learned and combined; figures/tables and examples reportedly support the AUC-based results (10+ point gain) and the reconstruction task. Writing quality is good and related work is thorough, but presentation could be tightened around experimental reproducibility details (no mention of code or hyperparameters in the summary), which slightly reduces clarity for readers wanting to replicate results.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (composition of modifier property-profiles for fine-grained IsA extraction). The methodology and experiments follow a logical flow and limitations/assumptions are explicitly discussed, which aids clarity. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and reasonably novel perspective by composing modifier-specific property profiles to populate fine-grained IsA classes and—crucially—applying noun-compound style semantic interpretations directly to downstream IsA extraction, a usage the authors claim is first-of-its-kind. While the high-level goal (taxonomy/IsA induction) has substantial prior work (e.g., taxonomy induction from heterogeneous evidence and classic hyponymy acquisition), the modifier-composition approach and the operationalization via property-profile matching plus reranking represent a non-trivial and distinguishable extension beyond those predecessors. Given the cited related work, the contribution is more than incremental but not a wholesale paradigm shift, so I rate it as notably novel rather than highly groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper offers a notable and plausibly new perspective by casting fine-grained IsA population as composition over modifier interpretations (property-profile distributions) and then applying these semantic interpretations directly to IsA extraction—a use of noun-compound-style interpretation for downstream taxonomy population that, to my knowledge, is not present in classic taxonomy induction work. While the high-level goal (hyponymy/taxonomy induction) is well-studied (e.g., earlier taxonomy induction and hyponym acquisition work), the specific formulation—learning per-modifier property profiles from OpenIE tuples and composing them to score unseen multi-word class labels—is a non-trivial and distinct contribution. The approach is therefore more than a simple combination of existing techniques, though it builds on substantial prior work in hyponymy and pattern-based extraction and has clear dependence on available extractions (a limitation noted by the authors).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and reasonably novel perspective by composing modifier-specific property profiles to populate fine-grained IsA classes and—crucially—applying noun-compound style semantic interpretations directly to downstream IsA extraction, a usage the authors claim is first-of-its-kind. While the high-level goal (taxonomy/IsA induction) has substantial prior work (e.g., taxonomy induction from heterogeneous evidence and classic hyponymy acquisition), the modifier-composition approach and the operationalization via property-profile matching plus reranking represent a non-trivial and distinguishable extension beyond those predecessors. Given the cited related work, the contribution is more than incremental but not a wholesale paradigm shift, so I rate it as notably novel rather than highly groundbreaking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and plausibly new perspective by casting fine-grained IsA population as composition over modifier interpretations (property-profile distributions) and then applying these semantic interpretations directly to IsA extraction—a use of noun-compound-style interpretation for downstream taxonomy population that, to my knowledge, is not present in classic taxonomy induction work. While the high-level goal (hyponymy/taxonomy induction) is well-studied (e.g., earlier taxonomy induction and hyponym acquisition work), the specific formulation—learning per-modifier property profiles from OpenIE tuples and composing them to score unseen multi-word class labels—is a non-trivial and distinct contribution. The approach is therefore more than a simple combination of existing techniques, though it builds on substantial prior work in hyponymy and pattern-based extraction and has clear dependence on available extractions (a limitation noted by the authors).

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a plausible method and reports strong AUC gains on reconstructing Wikipedia category pages, and it situates itself well wrt prior taxonomy/hyponymy work. However, experimental breadth and transparency are limited: evaluation appears confined to Wikipedia categories, the extraction repository D is in‑house (hindering reproducibility), and hyperparameters/code are not reported. Ablation/sensitivity analyses and clearer breakdowns isolating the contribution of each component (modifier profiles, composition rules, reranker) are not described, though limitations are explicitly discussed and related work coverage is strong. | B(3): The paper presents a plausible methodology and shows sizable AUC gains over a strong Hearst-patterns baseline on Wikipedia category reconstruction, but the experimental scope is limited (single evaluation corpus, single primary baseline) and there is no mention of ablations or sensitivity analyses for key components (modifier property construction, composition rules, reranker). Related work appears thoroughly covered (e.g., prior taxonomy induction and hyponymy acquisition efforts [1,2] are directly relevant), and the authors discuss some failure modes and assumptions, but reproducibility is weak: code is not mentioned, key resources are in-house extractions (ReVerb/OLLIE) and hyperparameters are not reported. Overall the evidence is adequate to support the main claim but missing important analyses and replication details to be fully convincing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible method and reports strong AUC gains on reconstructing Wikipedia category pages, and it situates itself well wrt prior taxonomy/hyponymy work. However, experimental breadth and transparency are limited: evaluation appears confined to Wikipedia categories, the extraction repository D is in‑house (hindering reproducibility), and hyperparameters/code are not reported. Ablation/sensitivity analyses and clearer breakdowns isolating the contribution of each component (modifier profiles, composition rules, reranker) are not described, though limitations are explicitly discussed and related work coverage is strong.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a plausible methodology and shows sizable AUC gains over a strong Hearst-patterns baseline on Wikipedia category reconstruction, but the experimental scope is limited (single evaluation corpus, single primary baseline) and there is no mention of ablations or sensitivity analyses for key components (modifier property construction, composition rules, reranker). Related work appears thoroughly covered (e.g., prior taxonomy induction and hyponymy acquisition efforts [1,2] are directly relevant), and the authors discuss some failure modes and assumptions, but reproducibility is weak: code is not mentioned, key resources are in-house extractions (ReVerb/OLLIE) and hyperparameters are not reported. Overall the evidence is adequate to support the main claim but missing important analyses and replication details to be fully convincing.

main_claims: Presents a method to populate fine-grained classes (e.g., “1950s American jazz composers”) with instances by composing interpretations of individual modifiers relative to the head.
Models modifiers as distributions over properties (property profiles) learned from extracted fact tuples and distributional similarity, enabling IsA extraction for classes unseen verbatim in text.

Demonstrates that the modifier-composition approach yields substantial empirical gains: over a strong Hearst-patterns baseline it achieves more than a 10-point increase in AUC when reconstructing Wikipedia category pages.
Introduces the first attempt (claimed) to use noun-compound semantic interpretations for downstream IsA relation extraction.
_summary.methodology: Learn per-modifier property profiles from a large repository of extracted fact tuples (from ReVerb and OLLIE) and distributionally similar modifiers; score candidate instances by matching instance facts to these property profiles (φ̂), combine multiple modifiers by intersecting candidate sets and averaging scores, and apply a weakly supervised logistic regression reranker.
datasets: Repository of facts D instantiated from in-house ReVerb and OLLIE extractions
limitations: Assumes relations between modifier and head that are discussed more often are more likely to capture important subclass properties (noted as imperfect).
Property-profile construction depends on available extracted facts and distributional similarity, which may miss relations not frequently discussed (Section 4.4 noted).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - IsA relation extraction / semantic taxonomy induction

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Empiricist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating large-scale NLP systems for information extraction and knowledge base population in both industry and academia.
traits: Insists on clear, reproducible experimental setups: dataset splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, and code availability
Prioritizes rigorous baselines and ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each component
Examines statistical validity: confidence intervals, significance testing, and sensitivity to hyperparameters
Values runtime, scalability, and engineering practicality (e.g., memory, speed, and data requirements)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and implementation transparency, asking for targeted ablations, error analyses, and reproducibility artifacts to substantiate empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Systems Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher specializing in lexical semantics, taxonomy induction, and compositional meaning (noun compounds and modifier interpretation) with a strong publication record on semantic resources and downstream applications.
traits: Evaluates novelty and conceptual contribution relative to prior work in noun-compound semantics and IsA/taxonomy induction
Assesses whether the proposed formulation addresses real gaps in the literature and would be useful to downstream tasks
Attentive to the appropriateness of evaluation tasks and metrics for demonstrating real-world impact and generality
Looks for clear discussion of limitations, failure modes, and connections to linguistic theory where relevant
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper by its conceptual novelty, fit within the research landscape, and potential impact, recommending experiments or analyses that clarify scope and applicability.
